title: Draught-proofing a cat or dog flap
subtitle: Reducing the heat loss through a pet door
excerpt: A standard cat flap with a single plastic flap leaks the equivalent of leaving a 150 mm hole in the wall open for several minutes every hour. Magnetic seals, brush strips, and double-flap kits reduce this without deterring the cat.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance for air infiltration figures. AECB technical notes on door and window sealing for background on magnetic-seal performance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Sealing gaps around doors, windows, and penetrations to prevent uncontrolled air infiltration. The cheapest and fastest-payback retrofit in most UK homes.

content.text: A standard single-flap cat flap sits in a hole cut through an external door or wall. The flap itself is a 150-200 mm square of plastic that relies on gravity and a magnetic catch to close. Air pressure differences across the door push the flap open intermittently, and even when closed, the seal around the perimeter of the frame leaks. A typical cat flap in an average UK home accounts for roughly 100-150 kWh per year in heat loss;
draught stripping
the frame and fitting a double-flap or brush-seal replacement reduces that to 20-40 kWh per year.
Assessing your existing cat flap

Hold a stick of incense or a damp finger near the frame seal on a windy day. If you feel or see movement, the frame is leaking. Push the flap and listen for the magnetic catch: if it does not click firmly, the magnets are weak. Look through the flap from inside in daylight: if you see light around the frame perimeter rather than a solid seal, the frame is not seated correctly.
Options
Option 1: Reseal the existing frame

Remove the existing flap body by unscrewing the four corner screws from the tunnel section. Clean the channel around the cutout with a scraper. Apply a bead of low-modulus silicone sealant around the external flange before refitting. Retighten the screws, working in a cross pattern. This addresses frame leakage but not flap leakage.
Option 2: Fit a double-flap replacement

Double-flap cat flaps have an outer flap and an inner flap separated by a 40-60 mm tunnel. Even when both flaps are momentarily open as the cat passes, the direct air path from outside to inside is broken. The air must change direction twice to pass through. This reduces infiltration by roughly 60-70 percent compared to a single-flap unit in the same hole.
Option 3: Microchip or magnetic exclusion

Microchip-activated cat flaps keep the flap locked until the cat approaches; magnetic-collar units unlock when the collar magnet is near. Both types are sealed when in the locked position. The closed-state seal is significantly better than a gravity flap, and the flap opens only briefly. These are the highest-performing options for heat loss, but they require a microchip-paired setup and the collar magnet or collar microchip reader.
Fitting a double-flap replacement
Remove the existing cat flap. Unscrew the four screws securing the outer flange. Pull the tunnel body through the hole toward you. Clean the cutout edges of old sealant.

Measure the hole dimensions: width and height in mm, and tunnel depth (door or wall thickness). Confirm the replacement unit's tunnel length covers the door thickness with at least 10 mm overlap each end.

Apply sealant to the outer flange lip. Insert the tunnel from the outside. Fit the inner flange from inside and secure with the supplied screws. Do not overtighten: plastic flanges crack if the screw torque is too high.

Check both flaps close fully under their own weight. Adjust the hinge tension if the unit supplies an adjustment screw. Test the magnetic catch by holding the flap half open and releasing it: it should close in under two seconds without assistance.
Wall-mounted cat flaps

A cat flap through a 220 mm solid-brick wall needs an extended tunnel. The standard tunnel sections are 40-70 mm; extension sections in 50 mm increments are available for most brands. A full solid-brick installation typically requires two or three extension pieces plus the standard tunnel. The multiple joints must be sealed with sealant at each junction. The inner and outer flanges are fitted as with a door installation.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Cat refuses to use the new flap
attrs.cause: Magnetic or microchip-activated lock confusing the cat, or tunnel length changed
attrs.fix: Tape the flap open for several days to let the cat re-acclimatise to the smell and feel of the new unit. Gradually allow the flap to close further each day. For microchip units, confirm the cat's microchip number is enrolled in the unit.

attrs.symptom: Draught still felt after fitting the new flap
attrs.cause: Frame-to-door gap leaking around the perimeter
attrs.fix: Run a bead of flexible draught-seal tape around the outer flange perimeter. Press firmly and check the adhesion is continuous with no bubbles.
